item:
Concept: “archived (bookmark boolean property)”
Statement: Whenever someone or something is archived, an archiving happened — something took place in time by which it was put away for keeping. Normally this putting-away is done on purpose, so that the thing is kept but out of the way. Typically there is someone — or a tool or system acting for someone — that does the archiving, the thing archived, and a place or collection where it is kept.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:archived

item:
Concept: “begin (happening X begins at time T)”
Statement: In every case of “begin (happening X begins at time T)” as described above, X is something that happens or goes on in time (a happening or process) — not a person, a place, or a time. Normally X is the kind of happening that lasts for some time once it has begun.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:begin

item:
Concept: “begin (happening X begins at time T)”
Statement: In every case of “begin (happening X begins at time T)” as described above, T is a time — a moment or stretch of time. Normally T is thought of as the moment the happening starts.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:begin

item:
Concept: “believe (X believes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “believe (X believes Y)” as described above, X is someone or something that exists on its own and lasts through time — not a time, a place, or a mere happening. Normally X is someone who can think — a person, or a being or group with a mind. Typically X is the one in whose mind the believing happens (the believer).
subject: urn:kernel-v0:believe

item:
Concept: “believe (X believes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “believe (X believes Y)” as described above, Y is something that can be thought — a content, the kind of thing that could also be put into words. Normally Y is something that can be so or not so — the kind of content that can be true or false. Typically Y, the content believed, can be about anything — a person, a place, a thing, or another happening.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:believe

item:
Concept: “birth (the event)”
Statement: Every birth is a happening — something that takes place in time (an event), not a lasting object. Normally a birth is a bodily happening in the life of a living thing. Typically a birth involves the one who is born (a child or young), and it happens at some time and place.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:birth

item:
Concept: “birth (the event)”
Statement: In every case of a birth, it happens at some time — there is a time at which the birth takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:birth

item:
Concept: “bookmark”
Statement: Every bookmark is a kept mark or record — something that stands for something else so that someone can come back to it; it is not itself a happening or a time. Normally a bookmark is a small piece of stored information — a mark, a note, or an entry that carries what it says in signs or words. Typically a bookmark is a record kept about something — the page or place it points back to.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:bookmark

item:
Concept: “bookmark”
Statement: In every case of a bookmark, it was made — a making or saving of it happened at some time. Normally it is made on purpose, so that something can be found again. Typically there is someone — or a tool or program acting for someone — that makes or keeps the bookmark.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:bookmark

item:
Concept: “bookmark”
Statement: In every case of a bookmark, there is something the bookmark marks or points to — something other than the bookmark itself. Normally what it points to is a place in something made of words or signs — a page, a spot, an address. Typically the marked thing exists separately and can be gone back to again and again.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:bookmark

item:
Concept: “break (X breaks Y)”
Statement: In every case of “break (X breaks Y)” as described above, X is what makes the breaking happen — someone or something. Normally X is someone — a person or other living being — acting on the thing. But X can also be a thing or a happening that causes the breaking — a storm, a fall, a blow.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:break

item:
Concept: “break (X breaks Y)”
Statement: In every case of “break (X breaks Y)” as described above, Y is a thing that can be in one piece and then not — something with a body or material to it, not a time and not a bare idea. Normally Y is a whole object — one thing that comes apart or stops holding together. Typically the breaking leaves pieces, or leaves Y no longer working as it should.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:break

item:
Concept: “break (X breaks Y)”
Statement: In every case of “break (X breaks Y)” as described above, the breaking happens at some time — there is a time at which it takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:break

item:
Concept: “broken”
Statement: Whenever someone or something is broken, being broken is a way that thing now is — a condition of it, not something separate from it. Normally it is broken because at some earlier time a breaking happened to it. Typically a broken thing no longer holds together or no longer works as it should.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:broken

item:
Concept: “cause (happening X causes happening Y)”
Statement: In every case of “cause (happening X causes happening Y)” as described above, X is something that happens or is the case — a happening or situation, not a lasting object, a place, or a time. Normally X is itself a happening — something going on that makes the other happening come about. Typically one can point to X as the answer to why Y happened.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:cause

item:
Concept: “cause (happening X causes happening Y)”
Statement: In every case of “cause (happening X causes happening Y)” as described above, Y is something that happens or comes about — a happening or outcome, not a lasting object, a place, or a time. Normally Y is a happening that would not have come about, or not in that way, without X. Typically Y comes after X, or while X is going on — not before.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:cause

item:
Concept: “cause (happening X causes happening Y)”
Statement: In every case of “cause (happening X causes happening Y)” as described above, the causing happens at some time — there is a time at which it takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:cause

item:
Concept: “celebration”
Statement: Every celebration is a happening — something people do that takes place in time (an event). Normally it is a social happening: people together doing something because something good happened or matters to them. Typically there are people taking part — those attending or celebrating.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:celebration

item:
Concept: “celebration”
Statement: In every case of a celebration, something happens — there are things done as part of it. Normally these are things people do on purpose — the celebrating itself. Typically this includes doings shared by those taking part.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:celebration

item:
Concept: “change (the event)”
Statement: In every case of a change, there is something that changes — something that was one way before, and another way after. Normally that something is there both before and after — it lasts through the change. Typically one can say what it was like at first and what it became.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:change

item:
Concept: “change (the event)”
Statement: In every case of a change, it happens at some time — there is a time at which the change takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:change

item:
Concept: “condolence (the words)”
Statement: Every condolence is something said — words, spoken or written, offered to someone. Normally the words say that the one offering them feels for someone over something bad — most often a death or a loss. Typically there is a message with a content, and a way it is given — said, written, or sent.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:condolence

item:
Concept: “condolence (the words)”
Statement: In every case of a condolence, the words are offered to someone — there is one the condolence is for. Normally that one is a person other than the one speaking — the bereaved or the one suffering the loss. Typically the words are addressed to that person directly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:condolence

item:
Concept: “condolence (the words)”
Statement: In every case of a condolence, someone offers the words — there is a speaker or writer of them. Normally a person, or people, expressing their sympathy. Typically the one offering it feels, or at least expresses, sorrow for the other.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:condolence

item:
Concept: “conversation”
Statement: Every conversation is a happening — an exchange that takes place in time (an event). Normally it is a social happening: people speaking with one another. Typically there are two or more people taking turns saying things to each other.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:conversation

item:
Concept: “conversation”
Statement: In every case of a conversation, words are said to someone — those speaking are speaking to someone. Normally to someone else: a conversation has more than one person in it. Typically each person in it is in turn a speaker and a listener.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:conversation

item:
Concept: “conversation”
Statement: In every case of a conversation, someone says things — there is at least one person speaking. Normally the ones in it are persons speaking with words. Typically more than one of them speaks over its course.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:conversation

item:
Concept: “death (the event)”
Statement: Every death is a happening — something that takes place in time (an event). Normally it is the ending of a life — a bodily happening in a living thing. Typically there is the one who dies, and a time at which it happens.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:death

item:
Concept: “death (the event)”
Statement: In every case of a death, something living dies — there is one whose death it is. Normally a living thing — a person, an animal, a plant. Typically we speak of the death of someone, or of some living thing, in particular.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:death

item:
Concept: “death (the event)”
Statement: In every case of a death, it happens at some time — there is a time at which the death takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:death

item:
Concept: “end (happening X ends at time T)”
Statement: In every case of “end (happening X ends at time T)” as described above, X is something that happens or goes on in time (a happening or process) — not a person, a place, or a time. Normally X is the kind of happening that was going on for some time before it ended. Typically after T, the happening X is no longer going on.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:end

item:
Concept: “end (happening X ends at time T)”
Statement: In every case of “end (happening X ends at time T)” as described above, T is a time — a moment or stretch of time. Normally T is thought of as the moment the happening stops. Typically nothing of X goes on after T.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:end

item:
Concept: “event”
Statement: In every case of a event, it happens at some time — there is a time at which the event takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly. Typically it also happens somewhere — there is a place to it as well as a time.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:event

item:
Concept: “find (X finds Y)”
Statement: In every case of “find (X finds Y)” as described above, X is the one that does the finding — someone or something that can look for things or come upon them; not a time or a place. Normally X is a person, or an animal or other being that can perceive. Typically X was looking for Y, or at least comes to know where Y is.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:find

item:
Concept: “find (X finds Y)”
Statement: In every case of “find (X finds Y)” as described above, Y is what is found — something that was not in view, or not known to be where it is, and then is. Normally Y is a thing, but it can be a person, an animal, a place, or even something like an answer. Typically Y is somewhere — the finding fixes where it is.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:find

item:
Concept: “forget (X forgets Y)”
Statement: In every case of “forget (X forgets Y)” as described above, X is the one who forgets — someone, or some being, that could know or remember things; not a time, a place, or a mere thing. Normally X is a person or other thinking being. Typically Y was in X’s mind before, and is not there when it is wanted.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:forget

item:
Concept: “forget (X forgets Y)”
Statement: In every case of “forget (X forgets Y)” as described above, Y is what is forgotten — something that was known, remembered, or meant to be done. Normally Y is something that can be held in mind: a fact, a name, a plan, a happening. Typically Y can also be a person, a thing, or a place, remembered under some description.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:forget

item:
Concept: “friend (X is a friend of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “friend (X is a friend of Y)” as described above, X is a being one can have a bond with — someone, not a mere thing, a time, or a place. Normally X is a person; people also speak of animals, or of groups, as friends. Typically X is one of two or more who like each other and are close.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:friend

item:
Concept: “friend (X is a friend of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “friend (X is a friend of Y)” as described above, Y is a being one can have a bond with — someone, not a mere thing, a time, or a place. Normally Y is a person; people also speak of animals, or of groups, as friends. Typically Y is one of two or more who like each other and are close.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:friend

item:
Concept: “gift”
Statement: In every case of a gift, there is someone the gift is for — one meant to have it. Normally someone other than the giver, and the giver wants them to have it. Typically that one receives it, or is meant to receive it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:gift

item:
Concept: “gift”
Statement: In every case of a gift, a giving is in play — the gift is given, or meant to be given. Normally the giving is done freely, with nothing owed for it. Typically there is the giver, the gift itself, and the one it goes to.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:gift

item:
Concept: “give (X gives Y to someone)”
Statement: In every case of “give (X gives Y to someone)” as described above, X is the giver — the one from whom Y goes; someone or something that can have and hand over. Normally X is a person or a group of people. Typically X has Y first, and after the giving the other one has it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:give

item:
Concept: “give (X gives Y to someone)”
Statement: In every case of “give (X gives Y to someone)” as described above, Y is what is given — something that can go from one to another. Normally Y is a thing that can be had or kept. But Y can also be something like help, time, words, or care.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:give

item:
Concept: “give (X gives Y to someone)”
Statement: In every case of “give (X gives Y to someone)” as described above, the giving happens at some time — there is a time at which it takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:give

item:
Concept: “grieving”
Statement: Whenever someone or something is grieving, they feel something very bad over one that is gone — grieving is a state they are in. Normally the grieving follows a death — of a person or other loved being — or another deep loss, and the loss came before the grieving.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:grieving

item:
Concept: “has part (X has part Y)”
Statement: In every case of “has part (X has part Y)” as described above, X is a whole — something with parts; it can be a thing, a person, a place, or even a happening. Normally X is one thing whose parts belong to it and make it up. Typically X is more than Y: the whole is not less than the part.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:has-part

item:
Concept: “has part (X has part Y)”
Statement: In every case of “has part (X has part Y)” as described above, Y is a part — something that belongs to X and helps make it up; it can be a thing, a person, a place, or even a happening. Normally Y is a smaller piece or portion of the whole. Typically Y is where X is — the part is not somewhere else than its whole.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:has-part

item:
Concept: “help (X helps Y)”
Statement: In every case of “help (X helps Y)” as described above, X is what does the helping — someone or something that makes things better or easier for Y. Normally X is a person or other being acting on purpose. But X can also be a thing or a happening that helps — a tool, a medicine, a change.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:help

item:
Concept: “help (X helps Y)”
Statement: In every case of “help (X helps Y)” as described above, Y is the one helped — someone or something whose doing or situation the help is for. Normally Y is a person or a group of people. Typically the help goes toward something Y is doing or needs.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:help

item:
Concept: “inside (X is inside Y)”
Statement: In every case of “inside (X is inside Y)” as described above, X is something with a place — a thing, a person, or a stuff that is somewhere; not a time. Normally X is a thing or being that could also be elsewhere. Typically X is surrounded by Y — within it, not at its edge.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:inside

item:
Concept: “inside (X is inside Y)”
Statement: In every case of “inside (X is inside Y)” as described above, Y is something with an inside — a thing, a place, or a container that can hold or surround; not a time. Normally Y has bounds — walls, edges, or a surface — with room within them. Typically Y is bigger than X and around it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:inside

item:
Concept: “kind (gufo:Kind, sortal type)”
Statement: In every case of a kind, there are — or could be — things of that kind: a kind is a way things are grouped as being alike.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:kind

item:
Concept: “learn (X learns Y)”
Statement: In every case of “learn (X learns Y)” as described above, X is the learner — someone or something that can come to know; not a time, a place, or a mere thing. Normally X is a person or other being with a mind — an animal can learn too. Typically after the learning, X knows Y or can do Y.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:learn

item:
Concept: “learn (X learns Y)”
Statement: In every case of “learn (X learns Y)” as described above, Y is what is learned — something that can be known or done: a fact, a skill, a way. Normally Y is content one can carry in mind — knowledge or know-how. Typically Y can be about anything — persons, places, things, happenings.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:learn

item:
Concept: “lie (the words)”
Statement: Every lie is something said — made of words, spoken or written. Normally the words say something the one saying them takes to be not true. Typically it is said to someone, about something.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:lie

item:
Concept: “lie (the words)”
Statement: In every case of a lie, the words are said to someone — a lie is told. Normally to someone else — though one can, in a stretched way, lie to oneself. Typically the teller wants the one told to take the words as true.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:lie

item:
Concept: “lie (the words)”
Statement: In every case of a lie, someone tells it — there is the one whose words they are. Normally a person speaking or writing. Typically the teller knows the words are not true.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:lie

item:
Concept: “lose (X loses Y)”
Statement: In every case of “lose (X loses Y)” as described above, X is the one who loses — someone or something that had Y and then does not. Normally X is a person, or a group, to whom Y belonged or mattered. Typically X can no longer find Y, or no longer has it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:lose

item:
Concept: “lose (X loses Y)”
Statement: In every case of “lose (X loses Y)” as described above, Y is what is lost — something X had, held, or kept. Normally Y is a thing that can be had — but it can also be a person kept track of, or something like time, a game, or a way. Typically after the losing, X does not know where Y is, or no longer has it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:lose

item:
Concept: “lost”
Statement: Whenever someone or something is lost, being lost is how things now stand with it — it cannot be found by, or does not know its way to, those it matters to. Normally this came about through an earlier losing — someone lost it, or it lost its way. Typically no one who matters to it knows where it is.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:lost

item:
Concept: “make (X makes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “make (X makes Y)” as described above, X is the maker — what brings Y into being; someone or something. Normally X is a person, or people, working on purpose. But X can also be a tool, a machine, or a happening in the world that makes something.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:make

item:
Concept: “make (X makes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “make (X makes Y)” as described above, Y is what is made — something that comes to be through the making. Normally Y is a thing one can point to afterwards — an object, a stuff, a work. But Y can also be words, a plan, or a happening — one can make a promise, or make trouble.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:make

item:
Concept: “make (X makes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “make (X makes Y)” as described above, the making happens at some time — there is a time at which it takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:make

item:
Concept: “maker of (X is the maker of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “maker of (X is the maker of Y)” as described above, X is the maker of Y — the one that brought Y into being. Normally X is a person or people. But a maker can also be a firm, a machine, or nature at work.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:maker-of

item:
Concept: “maker of (X is the maker of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “maker of (X is the maker of Y)” as described above, Y is what was made — something that came to be through X’s making. Normally Y is a thing one can point to — an object, a stuff, a work. But Y can also be words, a plan, or a happening that X brought about.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:maker-of

item:
Concept: “maker of (X is the maker of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “maker of (X is the maker of Y)” as described above, a making happened — Y came to be through it, at some time. Normally the making was done by X on purpose. Typically the making came before Y’s being there.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:maker-of

item:
Concept: “memory (a stored something)”
Statement: In every case of a memory, the memory is of something — there is something it keeps or is about. Normally something from before — a happening, a person, a place, a fact — as it was taken in. Typically the one whose memory it is can bring that content back to mind.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:memory

item:
Concept: “near (X is near Y)”
Statement: In every case of “near (X is near Y)” as described above, X is something with a place — a thing, a person, or a place itself; not a time. Normally X is a thing or being located somewhere. Typically the distance between X and Y is small, as such things go.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:near

item:
Concept: “near (X is near Y)”
Statement: In every case of “near (X is near Y)” as described above, Y is something with a place — a thing, a person, or a place itself; not a time. Normally Y is a thing, a being, or a spot that other things can be close to. Typically the distance between X and Y is small, as such things go.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:near

item:
Concept: “part of (X is part of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “part of (X is part of Y)” as described above, X is a part — something that belongs to Y and helps make it up; it can be a thing, a person, a place, or even a happening. Normally X is a smaller piece or portion of the whole. Typically X is where Y is — the part is not somewhere else than its whole.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:part-of

item:
Concept: “part of (X is part of Y)”
Statement: In every case of “part of (X is part of Y)” as described above, Y is a whole — something the part belongs to; it can be a thing, a person, a place, or even a happening. Normally Y is one thing whose parts belong to it and make it up. Typically Y is not less than X: the whole is at least its part.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:part-of

item:
Concept: “promise (the words)”
Statement: Every promise is something said — made of words, spoken, written, or clearly signalled. Normally the words bind the one saying them to do, or not do, something. Typically it is given to someone, about something to come.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:promise

item:
Concept: “promise (the words)”
Statement: In every case of a promise, it is made to someone — there is one to whom the word is given. Normally someone else; one can also promise oneself. Typically the one promised can then count on the thing promised.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:promise

item:
Concept: “promise (the words)”
Statement: In every case of a promise, someone makes it — there is the one who gives their word. Normally a person, or a group speaking as one. Typically the maker of the promise is then bound by it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:promise

item:
Concept: “remember (X remembers Y)”
Statement: In every case of “remember (X remembers Y)” as described above, X is the one who remembers — someone, or some being, in whose mind Y is kept or comes back; not a time, a place, or a mere thing. Normally X is a person or other being with a mind. Typically Y was known to X, or lived through by X, before.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:remember

item:
Concept: “remember (X remembers Y)”
Statement: In every case of “remember (X remembers Y)” as described above, Y is what is remembered — something that can be held in mind: a fact, a happening, a person, a place, a thing to do. Normally Y is content from before — something X knew, saw, or went through. Typically Y comes back to X’s mind as it was taken in.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:remember

item:
Concept: “reminder (the made something)”
Statement: In every case of a reminder, it was made or set up — something was done so that someone will call something back to mind. Normally by someone — a person — often through a tool or device that then does the reminding. Typically there is the one to be reminded, and the thing to be brought back to mind.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:reminder

item:
Concept: “reminder (the made something)”
Statement: In every case of a reminder, there is something it is meant to bring back to mind — the reminder is about, or for, something else. Normally something to be done, or something not to be forgotten. Typically that something is distinct from the reminder itself.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:reminder

item:
Concept: “repair (X repairs Y)”
Statement: In every case of “repair (X repairs Y)” as described above, X is the one that does the repairing — someone or something that works on Y so that it is right again. Normally X is a person, or people, with the needed skill. Sometimes X is a tool, a machine, or even the body itself doing the mending.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:repair

item:
Concept: “repair (X repairs Y)”
Statement: In every case of “repair (X repairs Y)” as described above, Y is what is repaired — a thing that was working or whole, then was not, and is worked on to be so again. Normally Y is a made thing — something built or put together that can go wrong. Typically after a good repair, Y works or holds together again.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:repair

item:
Concept: “repair (X repairs Y)”
Statement: In every case of “repair (X repairs Y)” as described above, the repairing happens at some time — there is a time at which it takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:repair

item:
Concept: “take (X takes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “take (X takes Y)” as described above, X is the taker — the one that comes to have or hold Y; someone or something that can do so. Normally X is a person or other living being. Typically X takes Y from somewhere, or from someone.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:take

item:
Concept: “take (X takes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “take (X takes Y)” as described above, Y is what is taken — something that can be had, held, or carried off. Normally Y is a thing; it can also be a person led along, or something like time or a chance. Typically Y comes from somewhere — a place, or someone who had it.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:take

item:
Concept: “take (X takes Y)”
Statement: In every case of “take (X takes Y)” as described above, the taking happens at some time — there is a time at which it takes place. Normally one can say when it happened, at least roughly.
subject: urn:kernel-v0:take
